class_schedule.topic: Foundations of Visual Design and Layout Systems
learning_objectives: Understand the principles of visual hierarchy in digital media
Identify and apply grid systems for structured layouts
Analyze effective composition in professional design work
readings: Making and Breaking the Grid by Timothy Samara, Ch. 1-2
The Elements of Typographic Style by Robert Bringhurst, Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: Welcome to Digital Design Principles. This course builds on your foundation in digital art to develop professional design skills used across branding, UI, motion graphics, and interactive media.
key_concepts: Grid systems: column grids, modular grids, hierarchical grids
Visual hierarchy: size, color, contrast, proximity, alignment
Gestalt principles applied to digital layouts
Whitespace as a design element
Responsive grid adaptation across screen sizes
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walkthrough of grid construction in Figma using a 12-column layout system applied to a portfolio landing page

review.value: Column, modular, and hierarchical grids provide underlying structure that organizes content and creates visual rhythm
The arrangement of elements to guide the viewer's eye in a deliberate sequence from most to least important

class_schedule.topic: Typography and Color Theory for Screens
learning_objectives: Select and pair typefaces for readability and brand expression
Apply color theory to create accessible digital palettes
Understand the relationship between typography scale and visual hierarchy
readings: Thinking with Type by Ellen Lupton, Ch. 2-3
Interaction of Color by Josef Albers, Plates I-VII

lecture_notes.introduction: Typography and color are the two most powerful tools a digital designer wields. Today we learn how to use them intentionally for clarity and emotion.
key_concepts: Type classification: serif, sans-serif, monospace, display
Typographic scale and modular scales (e.g., 1.25, 1.333 ratios)
Color models for screens: RGB, HSL, and their practical differences
Color accessibility: WCAG 2.1 contrast ratios (AA 4.5:1, AAA 7:1)
Building a cohesive color palette: primary, secondary, neutral, semantic colors
lecture_notes.demonstration: Creating a type scale and color system in Figma using design tokens, then testing contrast ratios with WebAIM tools
setup_requirements: Figma with design token plugin installed

review.value: A set of harmonious font sizes derived from a consistent ratio, creating visual rhythm and clear hierarchy
WCAG 2.1 Level AA requires 4.5:1 contrast ratio for normal text and 3:1 for large text against backgrounds

learning_objectives: Distinguish between a logo, a brand identity, and a brand system
Construct a brand style guide with defined usage rules
Apply brand consistency across multiple digital touchpoints
readings: Designing Brand Identity by Alina Wheeler, Ch. 1-3
Logo Design Love by David Airey, Ch. 2

lecture_notes.introduction: A brand is far more than a logo. Today we explore how identity systems maintain consistency and communicate values across every interaction.
key_concepts: Brand anatomy: logo, wordmark, icon, tagline, voice
Style guide structure: logo usage, color palette, typography, imagery, spacing rules
Brand applications across platforms: web, mobile, print, social
Scalability: designing marks that work at 16px favicons and billboard scale
Brand evolution and versioning strategies
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a mini brand guide in Figma: constructing a logo, defining color tokens, specifying clear space rules, and showing correct vs incorrect usage examples

review.value: A cohesive set of visual and verbal elements (logo, color, type, imagery, voice) governed by usage rules that ensure consistent recognition
The minimum protected area around a logo where no other elements may intrude, typically defined as a multiple of a logo unit (e.g., the height of the logomark)

learning_objectives: Apply the 12 principles of animation to UI motion design
Design purposeful micro-interactions that enhance usability
Specify motion parameters (duration, easing) for developer handoff
readings: The Illusion of Life by Frank Thomas and Ollie Johnston, Ch. 3

lecture_notes.introduction: Motion communicates relationships, provides feedback, and guides attention. When done well, it feels invisible. When done poorly, it frustrates users.
key_concepts: 12 principles of animation applied to UI: easing, anticipation, follow-through, staging
Micro-interactions: trigger, rules, feedback, loops and modes
Duration guidelines: 100-200ms for simple transitions, 200-500ms for complex ones
Easing curves: ease-in for exits, ease-out for entrances, ease-in-out for on-screen movement
Staggered animations for lists and grid reveals
lecture_notes.demonstration: Prototyping a card expansion micro-interaction in Figma with smart animate, specifying exact easing curves and durations in annotation layers
setup_requirements: After Effects or Principle (optional for advanced animation)

review.value: A contained product moment revolving around a single use case with four parts: trigger, rules, feedback, and loops/modes
Mathematical curves that control acceleration of animated properties; ease-out (decelerate) is standard for elements entering the screen

learning_objectives: Design layouts that adapt across mobile, tablet, and desktop breakpoints
Implement WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility standards in design deliverables
Use auto-layout and constraints for responsive component behavior
readings: Responsive Web Design by Ethan Marcotte, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Over half of global web traffic comes from mobile devices. Responsive design is not an afterthought -- it is a foundational design constraint.
key_concepts: Breakpoint strategy: mobile-first vs desktop-first approaches
Fluid grids and flexible images vs fixed layouts
Touch target sizing: minimum 44x44 CSS pixels (WCAG), 48x48dp (Material Design)
Semantic structure: heading hierarchy, landmark regions, alt text
Focus states, skip links, and keyboard navigation in design
lecture_notes.demonstration: Converting a desktop Figma layout to tablet and mobile using auto-layout, constraints, and component variants, then running an accessibility audit checklist
setup_requirements: Screen reader (VoiceOver on macOS) for testing

review.value: Starting the design process from the smallest screen and progressively enhancing for larger viewports, ensuring core content is always accessible
Web Content Accessibility Guidelines covering perceivable, operable, understandable, and robust criteria; AA is the legal baseline in many jurisdictions

learning_objectives: Architect a scalable design system with atomic design methodology
Build reusable components with variants, states, and documentation
readings: Atomic Design by Brad Frost, Ch. 2-4
Design Systems by Alla Kholmatova, Ch. 3

lecture_notes.introduction: Design systems are the single source of truth that keeps products visually and functionally consistent as they scale across teams and platforms.
key_concepts: Atomic design hierarchy: atoms, molecules, organisms, templates, pages
Design tokens: color, spacing, typography, elevation, motion as named variables
Component anatomy: base state, hover, focus, active, disabled, loading, error
Naming conventions and organizational structure for scalability
Documentation standards: usage guidelines, do/don't examples, code snippets
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a button component in Figma with 5 variants (primary, secondary, ghost, destructive, disabled), each with hover and focus states, linked to design tokens
setup_requirements: Figma with component library set up

review.value: Named entities that store visual design attributes (colors, spacing, typography) as platform-agnostic variables, bridging design and code
A methodology by Brad Frost that structures UI components in five levels of increasing complexity: atoms, molecules, organisms, templates, and pages

questions.question: A designer is creating a responsive web layout and needs to choose between a 12-column grid and a modular grid. Explain when each grid type is more appropriate, and describe how a 12-column grid adapts across a 3-breakpoint system (mobile, tablet, desktop).
questions.answer: A 12-column grid is ideal for content-heavy layouts like dashboards and editorial sites because columns can be grouped flexibly (1, 2, 3, 4, 6, or 12 columns). A modular grid is better for image-heavy or card-based layouts where both horizontal and vertical alignment matter. For breakpoints: mobile typically uses 4 columns with 16px margins, tablet uses 8 columns with 24px margins, and desktop uses all 12 columns with 24-32px margins. Gutters may also adjust at each breakpoint.

rubric.key: Correctly distinguishes use cases for each grid type
Accurately describes column collapsing across breakpoints

questions.question: What is the minimum contrast ratio required for normal-sized text to meet WCAG 2.1 Level AA, and why is this standard important for digital design?
questions.answer: The minimum contrast ratio for normal text under WCAG 2.1 Level AA is 4.5:1. For large text (18pt or 14pt bold), the minimum is 3:1. This standard is important because approximately 1 in 12 men and 1 in 200 women have some form of color vision deficiency, and low contrast also affects users in bright outdoor environments, on low-quality screens, or with age-related vision changes. Meeting AA compliance is also a legal requirement in many countries under accessibility laws such as Section 508 (US) and EN 301 549 (EU).

rubric.key: Correct ratio of 4.5:1 for normal text
Explains practical importance (vision deficiency, legal, environmental)

quizzes.topic: Motion Design, Responsive Layout, and Design Systems

questions.question: A mobile app displays a list of 8 cards that animate into view when the user navigates to the screen. Specify appropriate duration, easing curve, and stagger delay values, and explain why each choice improves the user experience.
questions.answer: Each card should use a duration of 200-300ms with an ease-out (decelerate) curve, such as cubic-bezier(0.0, 0.0, 0.2, 1.0). Cards should stagger with a 50-75ms delay between each item, giving a total animation time of roughly 550-825ms for all 8 cards. Ease-out is correct for entering elements because objects in motion naturally decelerate to rest. The stagger creates a visual cascade that helps users perceive the list as an ordered group rather than a sudden block of content. Keeping total duration under 1 second prevents the animation from feeling sluggish.

rubric.key: Correct easing curve choice with rationale
Reasonable stagger delay and total time consideration
Explains UX benefit of each choice

questions.question: Explain the atomic design methodology and describe how design tokens fit into a design system. Give a concrete example of a design token and show how it would be referenced in both a Figma component and a CSS variable.
questions.answer: Atomic design organizes UI into five levels: atoms (buttons, labels, inputs), molecules (search bar combining input + button), organisms (navigation header combining logo + nav links + search), templates (page-level layout structures), and pages (templates filled with real content). Design tokens are named variables that store fundamental design decisions. For example, a primary brand color token: in Figma it would be a color style named 'color/primary/500' with value #2563EB; in CSS it would be declared as --color-primary-500: #2563EB and referenced as background-color: var(--color-primary-500). Tokens ensure that changing one value propagates consistently across all components in both design and code.

rubric.key: Correctly names and describes all five atomic levels
Accurately defines design tokens with a concrete example
Shows both Figma and CSS representations

major_assignments.title: Brand Identity and Responsive Landing Page
major_assignments.description: Design a complete brand identity for a fictional product or service, then apply it to a responsive landing page designed at three breakpoints (mobile 375px, tablet 768px, desktop 1440px). The project must demonstrate mastery of grid systems, typography, color, and brand consistency.
objectives: Create a cohesive brand identity system from scratch
Apply grid-based layout principles across responsive breakpoints
Demonstrate accessible color and typography choices
Deliver design artifacts ready for developer handoff
deliverables: Brand style guide (logo, color palette with hex values, type scale, spacing system, usage rules)
Figma file with responsive landing page at 3 breakpoints using auto-layout
Accessibility annotation layer showing contrast ratios, heading hierarchy, and touch targets

criteria: Logo is clear, scalable, and works in mono and color
Color palette has sufficient contrast and includes accessible pairings
Typography selections are appropriate and well-paired
Style guide is thorough with correct/incorrect usage examples
Grid system is consistent and well-defined at each breakpoint
Content reflows logically across screen sizes
Auto-layout is used correctly for flexible components
Spacing follows a defined scale (e.g., 4px or 8px base unit)
All text meets WCAG 2.1 AA contrast ratios
Touch targets meet 44x44px minimum
Heading hierarchy is semantic and logical
Focus states are designed for interactive elements
Written rationale clearly explains design decisions
Brand choices are justified with reference to target audience
Technical decisions (grid, scale, tokens) are documented
Professional writing and presentation quality
Complete brand identity with professional-quality deliverables
Responsive layout demonstrating strong grid and hierarchy skills
Accessibility standards met or exceeded
Clear documentation connecting design decisions to principles

major_assignments.title: Design System Component Library
major_assignments.description: Build a functional design system in Figma containing a minimum of 10 components with full variant sets, design tokens, and documentation. The system must support both light and dark themes and include interactive prototypes demonstrating motion design principles.
objectives: Architect a scalable component library using atomic design principles
Define and apply design tokens for color, typography, spacing, and motion
Create components with complete state coverage (default, hover, focus, active, disabled, error)
Prototype micro-interactions demonstrating motion design guidelines
deliverables: Figma library file with 10+ components organized by atomic level
Design token specification document (JSON or spreadsheet format)
Interactive prototype showing at least 3 micro-interactions
Component documentation page with usage guidelines and do/don't examples
Dark theme variant for all components

criteria: All components have complete variant and state coverage
Naming conventions are consistent and scalable
Components use auto-layout and constraints correctly
Nested components are properly structured
Token naming follows a clear hierarchy (category/property/variant)
Tokens cover color, typography, spacing, elevation, and border radius
Light and dark themes are implemented via token swapping
Token documentation is complete and developer-friendly
Micro-interactions follow established motion guidelines
Duration and easing values are specified and appropriate
Animations enhance usability rather than decoration
Stagger and choreography are applied where appropriate
Each component has usage guidelines and context examples
Do/don't examples are clear and accurate
Accessibility notes are included per component
Overall documentation is organized and navigable
Comprehensive component library with professional organization
Design tokens are well-structured and support theming
Motion design is purposeful and well-specified
Documentation enables another designer to use the system independently

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of design theory, terminology, and critical analysis skills

lab_exercises.description: Hands-on Figma exercises applying layout, typography, color, and motion principles

major_assignments.description: Brand identity project and design system component library demonstrating integrated skills

participation_and_critiques.description: Active participation in design critiques, peer reviews, and class discussions

final_portfolio.description: Curated portfolio of course work demonstrating growth and mastery of digital design principles

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all critique sessions and lab workshops. More than 2 unexcused absences will result in a 5% grade reduction per additional absence.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work is penalized 10% per day. Design critique deadlines are firm because peer reviews depend on timely submissions.
course_policies.collaboration: Design critiques are collaborative. Individual assignments must be your own work. Inspiration sources and asset credits must be documented.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All design work must be original. Stock assets may be used only where explicitly permitted and must be credited. Submitting AI-generated designs as your own work without disclosure is a violation.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students must have a Figma account and reliable internet access. Adobe Creative Cloud is recommended but not required. Lab computers are available with all required software.
